figures.description: A clean, well-organized framework diagram illustrating a transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture with diffusion-like forward and reverse processes. figures.caption: Thoughtful color palette with semantic coding (blue for discrete tokens, teal for continuous hidden states, green for neural models); elegant use of dashed arrows vs solid arrows to distinguish forward/reverse processes; clean rounded component boxes; well-placed legend with intuitive arrow symbols; balanced asymmetric layout with clear information flow from bottom-left to top

figures.description: A four-stage framework diagram showing a data cleansing pipeline using LLMs. The figure presents: (1) few-shot CoT prompt design with input documents, (2) diversified reasoning paths with multiple LLM-generated rationales, (3) majority voting aggregation, and (4) final cleansed output. Uses custom icons, color-coded sections, and visual connectors to show data flow.
figures.caption: Clean horizontal flow with numbered stages; custom robot/LLM icons; color-coded reasoning boxes with dashed borders; visual majority voting representation with checkmarks; clear typography hierarchy; effective use of arrows and connectors showing transformation from noisy to cleansed data

figures.description: A well-crafted conceptual figure showing three scenarios of retrieval-augmented generation with custom-designed component boxes, icons representing LLMs and retrieval systems, and a vertical flow demonstrating how the proposed method refines incomplete or incorrect retrieved information into more concise, accurate outputs. The figure uses color coding, custom shapes, and clear visual hierarchy.

Features custom-designed colored block representations of sentences, scenario arrows, and clean architectural flow.

figures.description: A two-panel framework diagram illustrating a semi-supervised learning approach. The left panel (yellow-tinted) shows 'Pseudo Re-labeling' with a universal prompt feeding into a large-scale PLM, generating synthetic labeled data. The right panel (blue-tinted) depicts 'Supervised Contrastive Learning' with a Universal TAM module, momentum encoder, and denoising memory bank, showing how cross-entropy and supervised contrastive losses are combined.
figures.caption: Clean two-column layout with distinct color-coded backgrounds (warm yellow vs cool blue) separating methodological stages; custom rounded rectangular components with professional typography; dashed boxes effectively indicate data examples and memory storage; curved arrows and loss combination symbols add visual clarity; thoughtful use of color to distinguish positive/negative labels and different data flows

Stage 1 extracts acoustic landmarks (color-coded blocks), Stage 2 shows cross-modal instruction fine-tuning with a central LLM and four input types, and Stage 3 illustrates P-Tuning with prompt encoding and embedding matrices. The design uses rounded containers, custom icons, and a clear left-to-right flow with arrows.

The consistent board layout across steps enables easy comparison, while the blue/red highlighting system provides immediate visual feedback on reasoning quality. The hand-drawn style arrows and annotations add clarity to the complex logical deductions.

figures.description: The figure illustrates a framework for generating synthetic samples in rollouts using a large language model (LLM) and intrinsic rewards. It effectively visualizes the interaction between various components such as the world model (WM), encoder (Enc), and the generation of goals and rewards, making it a clear representation of the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 1: The algorithmic overall structure diagram of DLLM, where WM denotes the world model, ol represents the natural language caption of the observation, u denotes the transition, and ik corresponds to the intrinsic reward for the k-th goal. Consequently, we propose Dreaming with Large Language Model (DLLM), a multi-modal modelbased RL approach that integrates language hints
figures.paper_title: World Models with Hints of Large Language Models for Goal Achieving

figures.description: This figure illustrates the two-stage process of Joint Policy Gradient Optimization for a Retrospective Language Agent, highlighting both Imitation Learning and Reinforcement Learning components. The visual organization effectively conveys the relationships between different policies, losses, and environments, making it a valuable resource for understanding the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 2: Schematic of Joint Policy Gradient Optimization for Retrospective Language Agent. Our approach is divided into two stages: (a) Imitation Learning: We use expert models to generate expert trajectories, employ evaluators to filter out these trajectories, and then use them to fine-tune the student models. (b) Reinforcement Learning: The planner and reflector are jointly optimized through the off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm with the imitation learning regularizer.
figures.paper_title: Improving Retrospective Language Agents via Joint Policy Gradient Optimization

We built the WebUI interface using Streamlit7 , a powerful and user-friendly framework that allows for the rapid development of interactive web
figures.paper_title: CodexGraph: Bridging Large Language Models and Code Repositories via Code Graph Databases

figures.description: The figure presents a comprehensive analysis of attitudes towards various social groups through three distinct lenses: Minimal Set Analysis, Emotion Analysis, and Stereotype Analysis. Each section utilizes unique visual elements to convey complex data, including a word cloud for emotional responses and scatter plots for stereotype evaluations, making it an effective and engaging representation of the research findings.

figures.paper_title: What the #?*!: Disentangling Hate Across Target Identities

figures.description: This figure illustrates a structured approach to constructing a conversational dataset for text-to-SQL tasks, highlighting three distinct stages: database modification, SQL modification with clarification response generation, and refining the conversation. The visual representation effectively distinguishes between ambiguous and direct SQL responses, making it a valuable resource for understanding the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 1: An example of our conversational dataset construction consists of three stages: database modification, SQL modification along with clarification response generation, and refining the conversation. The top box depicts our data construction for an ambiguous question that requires clarification questions, while the bottom box illustrates an ambiguous question with direct helpful SQL responses. Here ‘U’ refers to a user and ‘A’ refers to a text-to-SQL assistant.
figures.paper_title: PRACTIQ: A Practical Conversational Text-to-SQLdataset with Ambiguous and Unanswerable Queries

It effectively uses visual elements to convey complex information in an accessible manner, integrating text and graphics to enhance understanding.
figures.caption: Figure 1: Multilingual naive RAG pipeline in Hindi (hn). In MIRAGE-BENCH, we reuse the oracle retrieval set (query and oracle judged passages) from MIRACL (Zhang et al., 2023) and focus on evaluating the answer generation stage with multilingual LLMs. reduce factual hallucinations with easy verifiability and improve faithfulness to passages provided within context (Khandelwal et al., 2020; Lewis
figures.paper_title: MIRAGE-Bench: Automatic Multilingual Benchmark Arena for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems

figures.description: This flowchart illustrates the MIRAGE-BENCH evaluation process, breaking it down into three distinct steps: heuristic-based features, arena-based evaluation, and surrogate judging. It effectively communicates the relationships between different models and evaluation criteria, making it a valuable resource for understanding the evaluation framework.
figures.caption: Figure 2: The MIRAGE-BENCH evaluation flowchart consists of three steps: (i) heuristic-based features evaluating the baseline model response across several dimensions; (ii) exhaustive pairwise comparisons with GPT-4o as a judge on a small subset of queries to train our surrogate judge. (iii) After training, we utilize our surrogate judge to output the model ranking on the whole subset of queries, to construct the synthetic RAG arena-based leaderboard. (Rackauckas et al., 2024; Pradeep et al., 20
figures.paper_title: MIRAGE-Bench: Automatic Multilingual Benchmark Arena for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems

figures.description: The figure presents a comprehensive framework for a simplification model using a pretrained large language model (LLM). It illustrates the process of generating complex sentences, sampling simplifications, and evaluating them based on user-specific vocabulary and sentence-level classifiers, highlighting the iterative nature of the model's learning process.
figures.caption: Figure 1: (better viewed in color) The overall framework of the proposed method: the simplification model is initialized from a pretrained large language model which is also used as a frozen ( ) reference model to provide entropy regularization (part 0.); top-k sampling is adopted in the decoding process to sample varied simplifications for the complex sentence (part 1.a.); the generated simplifications are evaluated based on the language proficiency level (vocabulary level and sentence level) o
figures.paper_title: Aligning Sentence Simplification withESLLearner’s Proficiency for Language Acquisition

figures.description: The figure provides a clear overview of the PeerQA data collection process, illustrating the steps from submitted articles to camera-ready articles, including peer review and question extraction. The flowchart format effectively communicates the methodology and highlights key expert annotations, making it visually engaging and informative.
figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of the PeerQA data collection process. From the peer review process (in green), we extract and process questions from the reviews. Given the published version of the article and a question, an expert (in our case, the original paper authors) (1) checks the question and modifies or discards it, (2) annotates whether it is answerable or not (i.e. if there is sufficient information in the paper), and if so (3) highlights the
figures.paper_title: PeerQA: A Scientific Question Answering Dataset from Peer Reviews

figures.description: This figure illustrates the FactMM-RAG system, detailing its four main components: Factual Report Pairs Mining, Multimodal Retriever Training, Embedding Space Search, and Retrieval Augmented Generation. Each section is visually distinct and effectively communicates the workflow and interactions between different elements of the system.
figures.caption: Figure 1: An overview of the FactMM-RAG system. It mainly contains three stages: (1) Leveraging RadGraph to characterize each radiology report and mine factually-informed report pairs; (2) Integrating factual knowledge into the training of the universal multimodal retriever; (3) Given the radiology image, employing the fact-aware multimodal retriever to search for factually-informed reference reports and augmenting the multimodal foundation model in generating accurate radiology reports.
figures.paper_title: Fact-Aware Multimodal Retrieval Augmentation for Accurate Medical Radiology Report Generation

figures.description: This figure presents a hierarchical classification of reasoning errors, categorizing them into three main branches: Insufficient Proof, Simplification, and Distraction. Each category is further divided into specific types of errors, providing a clear and organized overview of the different reasoning fallacies.
figures.caption: Figure 4: Taxonomy of fallacy types that we designed for evaluation purposes. Labels below the root (i.e., Insufficient proof, Simplification, and Distraction) represent the fallacy macro-categories for POST-C and SPAN-C tasks. we give them partial credit if the predicted label is an immediate parent of the actual label (Figure 4). This is achieved by setting δ = 0.5 as coefficient
figures.paper_title: Fine-grained Fallacy Detection with Human Label Variation

figures.description: This figure illustrates the concept of zero-shot learning, highlighting the ideal scenario of no label overlap between training datasets and benchmarks, contrasted with the problem of label overlap that can inflate performance metrics. It effectively communicates the challenges and ideal conditions for evaluating zero-shot transfer metrics.
figures.caption: Figure 2: With LLMs now capable of generating datasets that cover thousands of entity types, models trained on different datasets are subject to varying label shifts, making comparisons between them challenging. To address this, we introduce FAMILIARITY, a metric
figures.paper_title: Familiarity: Better Evaluation of Zero-Shot Named Entity Recognition by Quantifying Label Shifts in Synthetic Training Data

figures.description: The figure presents a case study on the Hybrid-QA method, illustrating its effectiveness compared to baseline approaches. It visually compares the performance of different methods in answering a specific question using a hybrid graph structure, highlighting the advantages of the proposed approach.
figures.caption: Figure 2: Case study on Hybrid-QA: Comparison of our method (ODYSSEY) against various baselines on an example from the Hybrid-QA dataset. Baselines: (i) Question + Context: Providing the LLM only the question without any additional context (ii) Question + Summarized Context: Passing the question along with the summarized documents and table. Our method delivers accurate answer because the Hybrid Graph efficiently connects "Street Fighter" from the document "Capcom" with the relevant table, guidi
figures.paper_title: Hybrid Graphs for Table-and-Text based Question Answering usingLLMs

figures.description: The figure provides a comprehensive overview of the ODYSSEY framework, illustrating the three main steps involved: Question Analysis, Hybrid Graph Construction, and Hybrid Graph Traversal. It effectively visualizes the process of extracting entities and linking them to documents, culminating in the generation of an answer using a pruned graph.
figures.caption: Figure 3: Overview of the ODYSSEY framework. Our method comprises of 3 steps: i) Question Analysis, ii) Hybrid Graph Construction, and iii) Hybrid Graph Traversal. First, we begin with Question Analysis (⃝1a in the figure) from where we get question entities, retrieved sub-table, and entity-header mapping. Next, we construct the Entity-Document Graph (⃝1b in the figure). Using entity-doc graph and retrieved sub-table, we construct the Hybrid Graph (⃝2 in the figure). At last, we perform Hybrid G
figures.paper_title: Hybrid Graphs for Table-and-Text based Question Answering usingLLMs

figures.description: The figure effectively visualizes the SEQAR framework, detailing the jailbreaking attack template and the automated optimization process. It uses clear labels and arrows to guide the viewer through the interactions between different models and components, enhancing understanding of the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 2: The overview of SEQAR framework. (Left): The jailbreak attack template of SEQAR. The character descriptions are generated and optimized in a greedy manner. During character optimization, the rth character is generated and evaluated with the previous r-1 characters fixed. The model response starts with these sentences . (Right): The automated generation and optimization process of the rth jailbreak character. The meta prompt is used to generate character descriptions with an attacker LL
figures.paper_title: SeqAR: JailbreakLLMs with Sequential Auto-Generated Characters

figures.description: This figure illustrates the learning by trial-and-demonstration (TnD) framework, detailing the three stages of training a language model. It effectively combines visual elements to represent the interactions between student models, teacher demonstrations, and the reinforcement learning process, making it a valuable resource for understanding the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 1: The learning by trial-and-demonstration (TnD) framework. In stage 1, we start by training a language model with the causal language modeling objective. In stage 2, we prompt the models along the learning trajectory for (text, step) pairs and train a neural age predictor to predict the training step given a text. In stage 3, we use the final model in stage 1 as the teacher model. In an interactive step, the student model is prompted to complete a trial, and the teacher model is prompted
figures.paper_title: Babysit A Language Model From Scratch: Interactive Language Learning by Trials and Demonstrations

figures.description: This figure effectively visualizes the architecture of the MoCE model, highlighting the roles of queries, keys, values, and the expert pool. It demonstrates how contextualization functions are treated as experts and how the router allocates them dynamically, making it a valuable resource for understanding the system's design.
figures.caption: Figure 1: This figure shows the overall structure of MoCE. All contextualization functions are treated as experts. The router dynamically allocates experts from the expert pool with their corresponding weights for each head of each Q, K, and V vector. The heads are locally contextualized with given experts, and then serve as the input to the following Scaled Dot-Product Attention module. and applies "Scaled Dot-Product Attention" for
figures.paper_title: MoCE: Adaptive Mixture of Contextualization Experts for Byte-based Neural Machine Translation

figures.description: This figure illustrates a multi-level approach to event extraction and causal relation discovery, detailing the hierarchical levels of mention, generalization, and abstraction. It effectively combines visual elements such as a causal graph and an abstraction matrix, making complex concepts more accessible.
figures.caption: Figure 1: Pipeline of abstract causal event discovery. An event is viewed from three hierarchical levels: mention (realization in a specific text corpus), generalization (conceptualization of the event’s components) and abstraction (group of causally consistent generalizations). Given a collection of event mentions, Phase 1 produces a collection of abstractions A, B, C that are mapped back to the original corpus to construct a suitable representation in Phase 2, such as a co-occurrence matrix. C
figures.paper_title: ACCESS: A Benchmark for Abstract Causal Event Discovery and Reasoning

figures.description: This figure illustrates the overall framework for prompt generation, response generation, and evaluation in a demographic context. It effectively combines various elements such as social scenario generation, demographic persona assignment, and response evaluation metrics, making it a valuable resource for understanding the methodology.
figures.caption: Figure 1: Overall framework for Prompt Generation, Response Generation, and Response Evaluation. We examined nine demographic axes Dk(k = 1, . . . , 9), each with 3–5 identities (Table 2), including race, gender identity, age, and religion. These axes were chosen for their relevance in representing diverse and marginalised groups. The pairwise combinations of identities across axes allowed us to explore both common and subtler biases, such as those related to physical appearance
figures.paper_title: Unmasking Implicit Bias: Evaluating Persona-PromptedLLMResponses in Power-Disparate Social Scenarios
